verbatim_text: So clearly it should be the case that these expressive adaptive algorithms should be much harder to learn than learning static models, which is what we were doing previously. So why is it really possible? It's possible now because pre-trained models come with really good priors for learning algorithms. So pre-trained models like large-language models or LLMs, they're trained on a vast corpus of internet data. And this is text data that represents the way how humans think and solve problems. It's in this text format. And now when you pre-train, these models pick up on these behaviors and patterns, and they come with these good priors for solving problems at test time, or they come with good priors for algorithm learning. Now with that prerequisite, I will state my broad goal, which is to train AI systems to continually adapt at test time and solve challenging test problems. My approach for this is rooted in this framework for algorithm learning. And I instantiate this approach in the setting of large-language models. I train these large-language models to be effective algorithms at test time through tools and techniques from reinforcement learning, which is nothing but a sub-area of machine learning that deals with sequential decision making. So why should we care about test time adaptation or learning algorithms? And as it turns out, algorithm learning can be extremely powerful. And to give you an example of this, I'll quote a result that we'll actually build up in the later part of the talk.
